name: Chicago, Illinois
tagline: The 3rd-largest U.S. city — ~2.7M residents on a $16.6B 2026 budget passed amid a ~$1.1B Corporate Fund gap. Enterprise IT runs through the Department of Technology & Innovation (DTI); buying is gated by the Department of Procurement Services and City Council ordinance.
overview: Chicago is the third-largest city in the United States — roughly 2.7 million residents — governed by a strong-mayor system under Mayor Brandon Johnson with a 50-member City Council. Enterprise technology direction sits with the Department of Technology & Innovation (DTI), the renamed and reformed central IT agency that sets citywide technology strategy, runs shared infrastructure and networks, delivers cybersecurity, and manages major technology programs for City departments; it is led by a Commissioner who also serves as the City's Chief Information Officer. Chicago is best understood as a centralized-but-departmental buyer: DTI owns enterprise infrastructure, security, data, and platforms, while large operational departments (Police, the Office of Emergency Management & Communications, Aviation, Water Management, Streets & Sanitation, Public Health) carry their own systems and modernization needs. The defining backdrop for 2026 is fiscal: the City closed a projected Corporate Fund shortfall of roughly $1.1–1.2 billion and the City Council passed a $16.6B budget in December 2025 — the first in city history crafted by the Council rather than the mayor's finance team — leaving officials warning the result is thin, with forecasts pointing to another large gap (~$1.16–1.3B) for 2027. For a Dell SLG seller this is one of the largest single municipal opportunities in the Midwest; the entry point is DTI's enterprise programs plus the Department of Procurement Services' competitively bid and cooperative vehicles, with most material contracts requiring City Council approval — not a single discretionary IT wallet.

budget.intro: Chicago's 2026 budget was shaped by a large structural deficit. The City forecast a Corporate Fund gap of roughly $1.15B for 2026, and after the mayor's spending plan stalled the City Council passed a ~$16.6B budget in December 2025, which Mayor Johnson allowed to take effect without his signature. The pressure does not ease: forecasts point to another projected shortfall of roughly $1.16–1.3B for 2027, driven partly by rising pension costs (total pension liability reported at ~$36.4B). DTI's own appropriation is funded mostly from the Corporate Fund (~69% of its proposed 2026 budget) with federal grants the next-largest source (~16%); software maintenance and licensing is DTI's single largest expense category — a signal that the application and licensing estate, not new build, dominates the technology line.

stats.label: 2026 city budget
stats.note: Council-passed Dec 2025; took effect Jan 1, 2026 without the mayor's signature

stats.label: 2026 Corporate Fund gap
stats.note: Projected shortfall the budget had to close; officials warned the result was tight

stats.label: Projected 2027 gap
stats.note: Forecast shortfall for 2027; rising pension costs (liability ~$36.4B) keep fiscal pressure on beyond 2026

stats.label: DTI top cost driver
stats.note: Largest DTI expense category in the proposed 2026 budget; ~69% Corporate Fund, ~16% federal grants

notes: The 2026 budget was the first in Chicago history crafted by City Council members rather than the mayor's finance team; the mayor declined to sign or veto it, and officials cautioned the budget was not fully balanced.

Fiscal pressure continues into 2027: city forecasts project another ~$1.16-1.3B Corporate Fund gap, with a rising pension bill (total liability ~$36.4B) a major driver — reinforcing a cost-takeout posture for technology.

DTI's budget skews toward maintaining the existing estate — software maintenance/licensing leads, with notable increases in IT maintenance and legacy telephone (Centrex) billings — pointing to rationalization and refresh conversations over greenfield spend.
IT spend is partly federated: large departments (Police, OEMC, Aviation, Water) run their own systems and modernization, so verify which department owns a requirement before forecasting.

initiatives.name: Generative AI roadmap & responsible-use policy
initiatives.summary: DTI, under the CIO, published a City of Chicago Roadmap for AI focused on generative AI, with guiding principles (equitable, inclusive, responsible) and recommended actions — a short employee acceptable-use policy, an AI center of excellence, and cross-department governance. A 2024 ordinance further directs AI programs under the CIO/DTI across areas like traffic, infrastructure, public safety, and constituent virtual assistants, with semi-annual reporting. The City now fields a First Deputy Corporation Counsel / Head of Privacy & AI, and city leaders participated in Chicago AI Week (June 2026) — an early, public lane for AI-governance, data-platform, and AI-infrastructure conversations.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity & enterprise resilience
initiatives.summary: DTI owns citywide information-security strategy across networks, enterprise systems, and infrastructure for a high-profile target — from O'Hare to regional water systems. The Information Security Office runs a shared-services model, and CISO Bruce Coffing has grown the security team from ~6 to ~24 budgeted positions since 2018 while framing AI as the major disrupter for both attackers and defenders. With a constrained budget and a heavy legacy estate, security operations, modernization, and resilience are a continuing, executive-relevant priority.

initiatives.name: Digital equity & broadband
initiatives.summary: Chicago's Digital Equity Council published a community-led Digital Equity Plan, and the Chicago Connected program — estimated at ~$50M over four years — provides free high-speed internet/hotspots to tens of thousands of CPS students, prioritizing the South and West Sides. The City is also pursuing neighborhood broadband and public Wi-Fi using city assets. Device, connectivity, and endpoint demand at community scale, often grant- or partner-funded.

initiatives.name: Legacy modernization & infrastructure refresh
initiatives.summary: DTI's budget points to an aging estate — rising IT-maintenance costs and a large legacy telephone (Centrex) line — alongside heavy software maintenance and licensing. The opportunity is consolidation, infrastructure refresh, and migration off legacy systems framed as cost takeout in a deficit year, not added spend.

procurement.intro: Selling to Chicago means navigating central procurement and the City Council, not a single buying desk. Competitively bid supplies, equipment, and services run through the Department of Procurement Services (DPS), where the Chief Procurement Officer is the contracting authority and vendors register and bid via the City's eProcurement system. DPS administers master agreements for hardware, software, and technology services via task orders, and the City can use cooperative ('piggyback') contracts to speed sourcing. Material contracts require City Council ordinance approval — build that timeline into any forecast.

vehicles.name: Department of Procurement Services (eProcurement)
vehicles.detail: The City's central procurement authority and e-procurement system for competitively bid supplies, equipment, and services; the Chief Procurement Officer is the sole contracting authority under municipal code. Register and bid here for City-specific solicitations.

vehicles.name: Hardware/Software/Technology master agreements
vehicles.detail: DPS administers master agreements for hardware, software, and technology services, buying via task orders against pre-established vehicles — often the fastest catalog-style path for IT once a usable master agreement exists. Confirm Dell's coverage and scope.

vehicles.name: Cooperative ('piggyback') contracts
vehicles.detail: The City can buy off cooperative contracts established by other governments or national co-ops where a usable, properly scoped vehicle already exists — a route to accelerate sourcing outside a full competitive solicitation.

vehicles.name: City Council approval
vehicles.detail: Material contracts and many technology awards require City Council ordinance approval. Map the Council/Committee on the Budget calendar and committee review into deal timing — a procedural gate distinct from the DPS award.

powerStructure.name: Brandon Johnson
powerStructure.title: Mayor of Chicago
powerStructure.note: Strong-mayor executive; DTI reports up through his administration. Note the 2026 budget was Council-led and unsigned by the mayor — the executive/Council dynamic shapes large technology investment decisions.

powerStructure.name: Nick Lucius
powerStructure.title: Commissioner & Chief Information Officer, DTI
powerStructure.note: Re-verified in seat as of mid-2026 (public appearances through May 2026). Confirmed CIO/Commissioner by City Council (48-0) in 2024; with the City since 2016 as data scientist, chief data officer, and CTO, plus a legal background. The top enterprise-IT relationship and owner of the AI roadmap.

powerStructure.name: Bruce Coffing
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer, DTI
powerStructure.note: Named CISO since 2018; a 2026 GovTech Top 25 honoree. Grew the security team from ~6 to ~24 budgeted positions and runs the Information Security Office shared-services model across City infrastructure (incl. O'Hare and water). The security buying-center owner — frames AI as the defining disruptor for defenders.

powerStructure.name: Sharla Roberts
powerStructure.title: Commissioner & Chief Procurement Officer, DPS
powerStructure.note: Appointed by Mayor Johnson (2024); leads the City's contracting, certification, and compliance operations. Sole contracting authority for City supply, service, and technology contracts; owns eProcurement, master agreements, and the path to a Council-approved award.

powerStructure.name: City Council — Committee on the Budget
powerStructure.title: Appropriations & contract approval
powerStructure.note: Drove the 2026 Council-led budget and approves material contracts and appointments — a real gate for both funding and award timing, not a rubber stamp in the current dynamic.

news.title: CISO Bruce Coffing named a 2026 GovTech Top 25; Chicago cyber team scaled from ~6 to ~24 positions
news.impact: Names and validates the security buying-center owner and signals a maturing cyber program under DTI — engage the CISO on security modernization, resilience, and AI-enabled defense, not just endpoints.

news.title: City AI governance momentum: Head of Privacy & AI role and Chicago AI Week (June 24-26) participation
news.impact: Confirms AI governance is an active, named executive lane under the Roadmap for AI — an opening for AI-infrastructure, data-platform, and responsible-AI conversations backed by comparable municipal references.

news.title: 2027 fiscal outlook: another projected ~$1.16-1.3B Corporate Fund gap; pension liability ~$36.4B
news.impact: Deficit pressure extends beyond 2026 — keep positioning technology as cost takeout (consolidation, licensing rationalization, infrastructure refresh) that reduces run-rate, not new discretionary spend.

news.title: City Council passes $16.6B 2026 budget; Mayor Johnson lets it take effect unsigned
news.impact: Closes a ~$1.1B Corporate Fund gap but is Council-crafted and thin — frame technology as cost takeout and modernization, and expect tight discretionary IT spend.

news.title: DTI 2026 budget hearing: software maintenance/licensing is top cost, IT maintenance and legacy telephone rise
news.impact: Confirms a maintenance-heavy, legacy-laden estate — an opening for infrastructure refresh, migration off legacy systems, and license/tool rationalization.

news.title: Mayor's FY2026 forecast cites ~$1.15B Corporate Fund shortfall
news.impact: Sets the deficit backdrop for every 2026 technology decision — prioritize consolidation, licensing rationalization, and refresh that reduce run-rate.

dellActions: Build the DTI relationship at the top — Commissioner/CIO Nick Lucius for enterprise strategy and the AI roadmap, and CISO Bruce Coffing for the security buying center — and confirm the CTO/deputy reporting lines before mapping buying centers.

Lead with cost takeout, not new spend: position ISG, infrastructure refresh, and license/tool rationalization against DTI's maintenance-heavy estate (software licensing, rising IT maintenance, legacy Centrex) in a multi-year deficit environment (2026 tight; ~$1.16-1.3B 2027 gap projected).

Tie AI-infrastructure and data platforms to Chicago's published Roadmap for AI, the 2024 AI ordinance, and the City's Privacy & AI leadership — bring comparable large-municipal references, not decks, as governance matures.
Engage the CISO's maturing program (team scaled ~6 to ~24; AI-enabled defense) on security modernization and resilience across a high-profile estate (O'Hare, water) — not a commodity endpoint play.

Get the procurement path right early: register in DPS eProcurement, work CPO Sharla Roberts' DPS on Dell's hardware/software/technology master-agreement and cooperative coverage, and build City Council ordinance approval into every deal timeline.
